observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management direct:
a. The Municipal Treasurer to include only checks already issued to the claimants in the RCI and to prepare a List of Unreleased Checks and cancelled checks which shall be submitted to the Municipal Accountant as basis for recording of disbursements; and
b. The Municipal Accountant to verify the entries in the RCI by examining the attached disbursement vouchers to ensure that the checks had already been released to the payees as evidenced by the acknowledgement in
Box E thereof. Also, prepare the necessary adjusting entries, by debiting the Cash in Bank – LCCA and crediting Accounts Payable for the disbursements which check was yet to be released to the payees.

We recommended that management require the GSO to conduct physical count of inventories at least every six months, prepare the RPCI and reconcile the same with accounting records. Also, instruct the Municipal Accountant to effect necessary adjustment in the book of accounts to recognize the consumed portion of the inventories and to present fairly the Inventory account balance presented in the financial statements.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
a. Require the Inventory Committee to conduct annual inventory-taking of all
PPE as of year-end and submit the RPCPPE in the prescribed form based
on the actual physical count conducted, together with the RPCLRN of the
LGU, also in the prescribed form, within the prescribed period;
b. Require the GSO-Designate and Municipal Accountant to properly prepare
and maintain the property records for all PPE and undertake the required
reconciliation of the Property and Accounting records thereafter;
c. Direct the Municipal Accountant to reclassify items with individual costs
below ₱50,000.00 to the appropriate semi-expendable property inventory
accounts in accordance with Section 3.3 of COA Circular No. 2024-006; and
d. Facilitate the One-Time Cleansing of PPE account balances through the
implementation of COA Circular No. 2020-006.

We recommended that Management:
a. Demand the immediate settlement/liquidation of outstanding cash advances,
particularly from the Municipal Treasurer and those who are still connected
with the LGU, pursuant to COA Circular No. 97-002;
b. Stop the practice of granting cash advances to officers and employees,
essentially the Municipal Treasurer, without a legally authorized specific
purpose, and instead directly issue checks for the payment of operating
expenses;
c. Require the Municipal Accountant to: i) strictly monitor the granting and
liquidation of cash advances and ensure that no additional cash advance is
granted unless the previous cash advance is fully liquidated or properly
accounted for, in order to avoid undue accumulation of outstanding cash
advances and ii) prepare and maintain an SL for each cash advance and
record the liquidation specifically to their corresponding cash advance to
avoid overlapping and improper offsetting of accounts;
d. Require the Municipal Budget Officer to record the obligation at the time of
granting of cash advance and not upon its liquidation to ensure timely and
accurate recognition of obligations;
e. Conform with the procedures and requirements set forth in COA Circular
No. 2016-005 and immediately facilitate the request for write-off of dormant
unliquidated cash advances, where applicable;
f. Require the Municipal Treasurer to prepare and submit RODs and LRs for
each cash advance drawn and stop the practice of submitting these reports on a
lump sum basis; and
g. Institute appropriate administrative and/or legal action against the AOs for
the immediate settlement of their cash advances. Otherwise, the Audit Team
will be compelled to exhaust the legal remedies for violating Article 217 and
218 of the Revised Penal Code and Section 89 of PD No. 1445 which is
punishable under Section 128 of the same Code.

We recommended that Management:
a. Require the Inventory Committee to conduct periodic physical count of all
supplies and materials on hand including the semi-expendable properties,
submit the required RPCI and RPCSP and coordinate with the Municipal
Accountant to reconcile the same with the accounting records;
b. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to properly record the purchase of
inventories in the appropriate inventory accounts and the issuance,
consumption and distribution thereof as expense upon receipt of RIS and
SSMI of a given period and make the necessary adjustments to reflect the
correct balance of the affected inventory and government equity accounts;
and
c. Require the GSO-Designate and the Municipal Accountant to properly
maintain, update and reconcile the SCs and SLCs, respectively. Also,
strengthen controls over the semi-expendable properties by establishing and
maintaining accurate records, reports, and registries as required by COA
Circular No. 2022-004.

We recommended that Management immediately facilitate the request for
write-off of the aforementioned dormant account balances in accordance with the
procedures and requirements of COA Circular No. 2016-005. Henceforth, strengthen
the monitoring, documentation and aging analysis of receivables to prevent the
accumulation of dormant accounts.

We recommended that the LCE:
a. Require the Municipal Treasurer to deposit immediately all his undeposited
collections and submit a copy of the deposit slip to the Office of the Auditor.
Otherwise, institute appropriate administrative charges against the
Municipal Treasurer for inability to deposit his collections within the period
required by law and regulations; and
b. Require the Municipal Treasurer to immediately submit the updated
cashbooks to the Office of the Auditor. Henceforth, regularly update his
cashbooks and ensure regular reconciliation of the same with the records of
the Municipal Accountant.

We recommended that the LCE:
a. Direct the Municipal Accountant to: i) give preferential attention in the
preparation and submission of the required BRS for all accounts and ii)
adjust the long-outstanding reconciling items in the books to correct the
amount presented in the FS and other documents; and
b. Require the Municipal Treasurer to consistently update all cashbooks to
ensure that the data reflected therein is accurate and can be reliably used in
the preparation of a complete BRS.

We recommended that Management:
a. Require the Municipal Accountant to: i) exert more effort to trace and verify
records related to dormant, unreconciled and non-moving accounts and
facilitate the return of any unutilized funds for completed/cancelled projects
to the concerned SAs and ii) consistently maintain an appropriate and
complete GL and SLs, including detailed schedules to ensure accurate
monitoring of balances under the Due to NGAs account and to establish
clear traceability of PPAs, thereby preventing future discrepancies; and
b. Institute the filing of a request to the Commission on Audit for authority to
write-off and/or adjust the dormant or non-moving accounts in accordance
with the procedures and documentation requirements prescribed under
COA Circular No. 2016-005.

We recommended that Management:
a. Instruct the Municipal Treasurer to: i) issue immediately the checks for
each LGU at an amount equivalent to their respective RPT shares and ii)
henceforth, ensure the prompt and regular remittance of the shares of the
concerned LGUs within five days after the end of each quarter; and
b. Require the Municipal Accountant to: i) consistently notify the LGUs of
their respective RPT share amounts each quarter to enable them to claim
the same from the Treasury Office and ii) trace and verify the prior years’
balances and prepare the corresponding supporting schedules thereof, for
fair and accurate presentation of account balances in the FS.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to
conduct a detailed review of the accounts and make the necessary
adjustments/corrections in the books of accounts to present the correct balances of
the affected accounts. Henceforth, exercise care in recording and classifying the
Municipality’s financial transactions to ensure that the balances of reciprocal
accounts are at all times the same.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant and the
Municipal Treasurer to immediately submit the lacking DVs with complete
supporting documents as well as the unsubmitted disbursement and collection
accounts for CY 2024. Strict adherence with the reglementary periods in the
rendition of accounts with complete supporting documents and necessary
financial reports, schedules and FS is also enjoined.

We recommended that the LCE direct the Municipal Treasurer to submit to the
Commission on Audit all the aforementioned cancelled and unused old checks for
validation and henceforth, ensure that all cancelled checks are attached to the RCI
and reported chronologically with the other checks issued with the word “Cancelled”
clearly indicated therein. Likewise, prudence must be exercised in encoding the
details of the checks in order to avoid wastage of checks/resources.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Budget Officer to
prepare and maintain RAAOs in the prescribed format by function/program/project,
allotment class, fund source and by current/continuing appropriations for proper
monitoring of balances of available appropriations and allotments. Likewise,
prepare the RAAOs for prior years accounts to check/verify the details of the
projects and balances of the continuing appropriations for submission to the
Audit Team. Further, ensure that the SAAOB is based on accurate and complete
records reflected in the properly maintained RAAOs.

We recommended that the LCE and the Local Sanggunian, with the assistance of
the Local Finance Committee, facilitate the revision/updating and enactment of the
Municipal Revenue Code in compliance with RA No. 7160 to improve the revenue
generation of the Municipality and provide more funds for its operations. Also,
incorporate all relevant ordinances enacted or amended from CYs 2018 to 2024 in
the revised Revenue Code.

We recommended that Management:
a. Require the LSB to ensure that the activities and timelines in the planning
and budgeting process are strictly observed as prescribed under DepEd-
DBM-DILG JC No. 1, s. 2017. Further, incorporate in the budget,
programs and activities intended for ECCD as required under Section 7 (b)
of RA No. 10410;
b. Direct the Municipal Accountant to immediately record all liquidated
transactions in the books of accounts, make the necessary adjustments to
correct all erroneous recording and ensure that the supporting documents
are proper and complete before processing the claim or payment of the
same; and
c. Submit an explanation on the noted deficiencies particularly on the grant of
cash advance prior to the approval of the SEF Budget.

a. Develop and Implement Written Policies and Procedures:
• Based on the customized procedures, draft a Cash Management Manual for
MGE. Use the NGAS Manual procedures as a framework but adapt them
to the MGE’s specific needs and challenges. This manual should include:
o Roles and Responsibilities: Clearly define the duties of collectors, tellers,
liquidating officers, the treasurer, and the accountant.
o Step-by-Step Procedures: Provide detailed instructions for collections,
remittances, deposits, and reconciliations.
o Forms and Templates: Include templates for RCDs, JEVs, and other
required documents.
o Timelines: Specify deadlines for remittances, deposits, and
reconciliations.
o Sanctions for Non-Compliance: Outline penalties for violations of the
policies (e.g., delayed remittances, unsupported disbursements).
b. Monitor and Evaluate Compliance
• Establish a monitoring system to track compliance with the policies. For
example:
o Conduct monthly examination of cash collections and deposits.
o Perform surprise cash counts to verify the accuracy of records.
o Review RCDs and JEVs regularly to ensure timely and accurate
reporting.
o Evaluate the effectiveness of the policies annually and update them as
needed.
• Ensure that reconciliations between Accounting and AOs are conducted at
least quarterly, or more frequently if required by management.
• Ensure that Bank Reconciliations are conducted monthly.
c. Address Operational Challenges:
• Provide secure transport mechanisms for field collectors to facilitate timely
remittances.
• Implement measures to reduce delays caused by travel time and distance.
d. Impose Sanctions for Non-Compliance:
• Establish a system of sanctions for violations of cash management policies
and procedures with a graduated system (e.g., warnings, suspensions) to
ensure adherence.
e. Conduct Regular Training and Dissemination:
• Conduct training sessions for all employees involved in cash management
to ensure they understand the new policies and procedures.
• Distribute the Cash Management Manual to all relevant staff and make it
accessible for reference.

a. Enforce strict liquidation policies:
• Require all accountable officers to settle outstanding cash advances within
a reasonable period.
• Suspend new cash advances for officers and employees with overdue
balances until full liquidation is achieved.
b. Strengthen internal controls:
• Enhance pre-approval review by having the Accounting and Treasurer’s
Offices verify the legality of cash advance purposes.
c. Prohibit cash advances for procurement transactions:
• Adhere strictly to procurement law by processing all procurement
transactions through established competitive bidding or alternative method
procedures and disbursing payments exclusively by check, in accordance
with the Imprest System.
d. Restrict cash advances for elected officials:
• Limit cash advances for elected officials strictly to official travel expenses
per COA Circular 97-002.
e. Enhance training and capacity building:
• Conduct regular training on PD 1445, COA Circulars, and procurement
laws.
• Hold accountability seminars to underscore the legal and fiscal
repercussions of non-compliance.
f. Implement disciplinary measures:
• Impose sanctions, such as salary holds or formal reprimands, for non-
compliant approvals or delayed liquidations.
• Incorporate compliance with cash advance policies into performance
evaluations and promotions.
g. Improve monitoring and reporting:
• Task the Internal Audit Unit with monthly reconciliation of cash advances
and aging balance reporting.
• Consider public disclosure of liquidation statuses to enable public scrutiny,
enhance transparency and mitigate political influence.
h. Institute cultural and procedural Reforms
• Leadership Commitment: The Mayor and department heads must lead by
example and publicly commit to strict compliance by issuing formal
statements through official memos and displaying them on the LGU
website.
• Zero-Tolerance Policy: Enact a municipal executive order imposing strict
penalties for violations, including administrative liability under existing
laws, rules and regulations.

a. Conduct One-Time Cleansing of PPE Accounts:
Implement COA Circular 2020-006 to resolve concerns related to PPE
accounts' accuracy, existence, and valuation. This one-time cleansing process
will involve the following:
• Conducting complete physical counts.
• Recognizing PPE items found at station.
• Disposing of non-existing/missing PPE items.

b. To ensure these issues do not reoccur, management must enhance
accountability within key departments by establishing disciplinary or
corrective measures if responsibilities are not met. In particular, the
Accounting Office, Property Office, and Inventory Committee must strictly
adhere to both IPSAS guidelines for accurate financial reporting and NGAS
Sections 119-124 for effective physical inventory management and
documentation. Key steps include:
• Clearly defining and monitoring responsibilities: Develop a detailed
responsibilities matrix that specifies each officer’s duties, reporting lines,
and deadlines. This should include defined performance indicators related
to asset record-keeping and inventory management.
• Instituting regular reviews and audits: Schedule quarterly internal audits
and monthly progress meetings to verify compliance with IPSAS and NGAS
requirements. This process should involve cross-checking physical
inventory counts against recorded data and documenting all findings for
immediate corrective action.
• Enforcing disciplinary actions: Establish a structured disciplinary
framework that outlines specific consequences for non-compliance. This
should include immediate corrective measures, mandatory retraining for
recurring issues, and escalation procedures to management for unresolved
deficiencies.

Further, maintain a separate subsidiary ledger for each project per barangay to easily monitor and track the liquidation of the downloaded funds.

We recommended to the management to formulate a comprehensive set of policies/guidelines governing the implementation of AICS program which must be aligned with the provisions of Memorandum Circular No. 02 s. 2014, to be approved by the Sangguniang Bayan.

We recommend that the Management direct the concerned officials to pass an Appropriation Ordinance providing specific allocation for the operation and maintenance of the PRDP subproject and open a separate trust fund account where the allocated fund will be deposited pursuant to Section 7.06 of the Implementation Management Agreement and Item 2.8 of PRDP Intensified Building-Up of Infrastructure and Logistics for Development (I-BUILD) Component Operations Manual.

Create an Inventory Committee who will conduct a systematic and complete physical count of PPE in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 and ensure timely submission of RPCPPE, complete with appropriate information on their conditions, to the Office of the Auditor; and

Require the GSO-Designate and the Municipal Accountant to maintain an updated Property Cards and Property Ledger Cards, respectively, per category on all PPE as recorded in the books of accounts, and continuously coordinate with each other in order to reconcile the discrepancies in the respective PPE accounts.

For missing PPE items which can no longer be found/located despite great effort, we recommended that Management strictly follow the guidelines of Section 7.0 of COA Circular No. 2020-006 for the disposition of non-existing/missing PPE items of government agencies.

measures are co- developed with management:

PPEs as a basis for recording in the book and providing
proper depreciation to all depreciable assets; and
c. Institute mechanism controls over the safekeeping of all
legal documents concerning LGU’s owned PPE.

Therefore, we recommended the following to the Municipal

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE):
Create an Inventory Committee whose members are capable, within the allocated time frame for physical inventory, to conduct a complete physical count of PPE in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 and ensure timely submission of RPCPPE to the Office of the Auditor;
Require the GSO-Designate and the Municipal Accountant to maintain an updated Property Cards and Property Ledger Cards, respectively, per category on all PPE as recorded in the books of accounts, and regularly coordinate with each other so as to reconcile the discrepancies in the respective PPE accounts; and
For missing PPE items which can no longer be found/located despite great effort, we recommended that Management strictly follow the guidelines of Section 7.0 of COA Circular No. 2020-006 for the disposition of non-existing/missing PPE items of government agencies.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Collecting Officers to remit their collections daily and intact to the Municipal Treasurer, otherwise, impose appropriate actions to compel them to diligently observe their obligation; and
Strict compliance to the provisions of Section 69 of P.D. No.1445 and Section 26 of COA Circular No. 92-382 must be observed.

We recommended that Management:
Direct the BAC to submit the documents required to be attached to the disbursement vouchers relevant to the infrastructure projects as supporting documents enumerated in Section 9.1 for the procurement through public bidding, Section 9.1.1 for infrastructure projects and Section 9.1.1.3 for progress payment of COA Circular No. 2012-001;
Remind the BAC and the Municipal Accountant that the submission of documents for contract review and for final inspection are different from the required attachments of the disbursement vouchers as supporting documents; and
Strict compliance on the completeness of the supporting documentary requirements before every disbursement voucher be approved for payment in order to ascertain the propriety and validity of all claims against the Municipality funds.

We recommended that Management:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to strengthen its internal control in the pre-audit of financial transactions and ensure that the supporting documents were complete and approved by the proper officials, otherwise, suspend the processing of transactions until the required supporting documents are completed and properly accomplished;
Submit immediately the lacking supporting documents of the aforementioned financial transactions; and
Consider processing every progress billing/final payment without deducting the Retention Money for convenience and reduce bulky documents provided that the contractor substitutes an equivalent retention money bond in accordance with Section 6.2, Annex E of RA No. 9184.

We recommended that the LCE:
Require the BAC to strictly comply with the provisions of 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184, particularly Section 47 Disclosure of Relations, in that the Bidder must fully disclose its relations to the Procuring Entity so as to ensure transparency and competitiveness in bidding; and
Ensure that contract entered into by the LGU is not contrary to RA No. 3019, and ensure that the winning bidder has no conflict of interest to persuade, induce or influence another public officer to perform an act constituting a violation of rules and regulations duly promulgated by competent authority or an offense in connection with the official duties of the latter, or allowing himself to be persuaded, induced, or influenced to commit violation of government procurement law.
Ensure that the BAC Members as well as other elected officials have no direct or indirect financial or pecuniary interest in any business, contract or transaction in connection with which he intervenes or takes part in his official capacity, or in which he is prohibited by the Constitution or by any law from having any interest.

We recommended that Management:
a.	Strictly comply with the submission of report on information and publicity of LGU PPAs as provided in COA Circular 2013-004; and
b.	Proper coordination and reconciliation by the concerned LGU Officials particularly the Municipal Engineer and Municipal Accountant, must be made in order to facilitate the preparation and submission of the required reports to the COA within due date.

We recommended that Management:
Ensure that the unspent current year LDRRM Fund amounting to ₱3,407,633.94 must be transferred to Special Trust Fund -DRRM; and
Ensure that the transfer of cash for the unspent DRRM balances which includes only the QRF and Mitigation Fund (MF)-MOOE must be made at the end of the year in accordance with Item 5.1.10 of COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended that Management:
Ensure that the PO must be properly and completely accomplished with all the necessary information or data as required in the prescribed form before processing the financial transaction especially in case of Negotiated Purchase which requires prior approval of the Sanggunian indicating therein the approved resolution number and signed/certified correct by the Secretary to the Sanggunian; and
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to institute adequate internal control in the pre-audit of financial transactions and devise means so as to ensure that any deficiencies noted in the PO will be immediately rectified by Management and expedite the processing of transaction.

We recommended that Management:
Strictly comply with the requirements of submission of PO and their supporting documents to the Commission on Audit within five working days upon its issuance pursuant to COA Circular 2009-001, and
Require the concerned officials or employees to provide a written justification as to non- submission of POs and their supporting documents to COA within the prescribed period, for the whole year of 2023.

We recommended that Management:
a.	Require the Municipal Accountant to submit the monthly reports, disbursement vouchers and its supporting documents to COA within ten calendar days of the ensuing month as prescribed in the rules;
b.	Hire additional competent staff or personnel in the Accounting Office, if the LGU has available budget, to cope with the voluminous transactions occurring each month in the Municipality so as to expedite the submission of accounts to the Auditor; and
c.	Evaluate the process of preparing the disbursement vouchers, RCIs, RCDs, RAAFs, and relevant documents and devise measures to prevent further occurrence of this deficiency in the succeeding years and be reminded that processing of vouchers must be undertaken when they were properly approved and with complete documentation.

We recommended that Management:
a.	Strictly comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2014-001 and PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2016-01;
b.	Require the concerned Officials and employees, particularly the GAD Focal Point person, to diligently observe the timely submission of GAD Plan and Budget and GAD Accomplishment Report to COA, as well as in other government agencies such as the DILG; and
c.	Require the Municipal Accountant to promptly submit the GAD related DVs to COA in order for the Audit Team to have an ample time to review the same.

We recommended that Management instruct the GFPS to:
Conduct a gender sensitivity analysis and implement programs and activities that specifically address gender issues based on the results of the analysis; and
Evaluate thoroughly the GAD PPAs that are incorporated in GPB, and charge to other funds, such as SEF, GF, LDRRMF, and other special purpose fund those PPAs’ that may not be considered as GAD related activities, especially those expenses in which no gender issues were addressed and merely for general purpose.

We recommended that Management, with the Municipal Accountant and
Municipal Treasurer as lead, to strengthen cash controls in accounting, recording and
reporting, to exact accountability and accuracy of CIB-LCCA by:

a. advising the Accounting Office to prepare the BRS within the
reglementary period or at least within a reasonable time and formally
documenting in writing the cash and reconciliation standards or
policies and key controls are in place and effective in addressing the
concerns affecting the accounting and recording of CIB-LCCA for the
complete, timely and accurate reporting; and
b. establishing a practicable and effective control activities by the
Treasurer’s Office to facilitate the timely transmission and/or
submission of DVs with complete supporting documents to the
Accounting Office and to the Audit Team.

We recommended for Management to adopt a holistic developmental
approach that focuses more on reducing disaster vulnerabilities to reinforce resiliency
and sustainability of DRRM initiatives than disaster response.

We recommended that Management, with the Municipal Disaster Risk
Reduction and Management Officer as lead, to revisit and enhance its current
monitoring and evaluation activities by having the related processes formalized and
appropriately integrating the principles of results-based management to reinforce the
delivery of its DRRM initiatives.

We recommended for the Municipal Accounting Office to consider the
recording of semi-expendable items in the respective inventory/expense accounts as
illustrated and eliminating the same from the PPE balances, and the corresponding
accumulated depreciation; and the General Services Office to issue the corresponding
Inventory Custodian Slip to all the end-users of the semi-expendable items, in view of
the gaps observed and raised in audit.

We recommended that Management, with the Offices of the Mayor, Municipal
Accountant and Municipal Treasurer as lead, enforce and implement the rules and
regulations pertaining to the grant, liquidation and/or settlement of cash advances to
exact accountability by:

a. requiring approval on the face of the cash advance voucher that said
official/employee has no previous unliquidated cash advance; and
b. issuing demand letter/s for advances already due and demandable and
withhold salary releases if the official/employee still fails to liquidate the cash
advance within the specified time frame.

We recommended that Management, with the Offices of the Mayor, Municipal
Accountant and Municipal Treasurer as leads, further improve the propriety in
handling its transactions through reinforcement of regulatory compliance by:

a. discontinuing the practice of granting cash advances to facilitate
transactions where the observance of the government procurement
processes is required; and
b. demanding the immediate settlement of the above subject advances.

Direct the Municipal Accountant to give preferential attention to the preparation and submission of the required BRS, prepare subsidiary ledgers per bank accounts, review the records relevant to the Cash in Bank account and effect necessary adjustments to correct the amount presented in the financial statements and other documents; and

Direct the Municipal Treasurer to update religiously his cash books and the data reflected be preferably considered in the preparation of the BRS to ensure that any reconciling items will be accordingly adjusted.

necessary processes to establish and reconcile the reciprocal
accounts, Due to LGUs and Due from LGUs, in order to come up
with reliable representations as regards the RPT shares for each
barangay of the municipality.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Office of the
Municipal Treasurer review its existing internal
practices/processes and, as far as practicable, strengthen measures
over cash collections, as well as establish an internal process that
affords for the timely forwarding of documents to the Office of
the Municipal Accountant to facilitate the prompt production of
reports that necessarily include revenue collections.

representation as regards its loans payable and other related

procedures.

projects for CY 2022;

Trust Fund book to record the on-going projects to
the appropriate CIP accounts; and

processes, taking into consideration the applicable
standards and laws, in order to reflect the correct
balances of each account.

Municipal GAD FPS-TWG, enhance the quality of its GPB and
delivery of its GAD PPAs by:

and keeping up-to-date its GAD Database through the
implementation and utilization of the Community-Based

building of GAD actors on nuances /matters/ principles/
technicalities such as, but not limited to, the RBM,
project management, and gender-issue identification that
affect GBP planning, implementation, monitoring,
evaluation, and reporting.

leads, to reinforce the application of the rules and regulations

recognize settlement of outstanding cash advances.

observations.recommendation:

date its GAD Database through the implementation and utilization of the

processes/tools; and
b. improving institutional knowledgebase through capacity building of GAD
actors on nuances/matters/principles/technicalities such as, but not limited to,
the RBM, project management, and gender-issue identification that affect GBP
planning, implementation, monitoring, evaluation, and reporting.

We recommended and Management agreed, with the Offices of the Mayor,
Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer as leads, to reinforce the application
of the rules and regulations pertaining to the grant, liquidation and/or settlement of
cash advances to exact accountability by:
a. requiring approval on the face of the cash advance voucher that said
official/employee has no preceding unliquidated cash advance;
b. issuing demand letter/s for advances already due and demandable and halt
salary releases if the official/employee still fails to liquidate the cash advance
within the specified time frame;
c. designating a Special Disbursing Officer (SDO) for advances for special
undertaking for proper control and monitoring of the grants and liquidations;
and
d. drawing necessary journal entries to appropriately recognize settlement of
outstanding cash advances.

We recommended and Management agreed, with the Offices of the Mayor and
Municipal Accountant as leads, to ensure that cash advances of those who are and
would-be separated from the service are settled by enforcing measures
provided/allowed by existing regulations, such as demands, clearances, and
withholding of salaries, to exact accountability.

1.10 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant coordinate with the
depository banks to request for copies of the credit and debit memos to
facilitate the validation of the reconciling items and immediate recording of the
required adjustments in the books. Also, to record the appropriate adjusting
entries for the stale checks in the GF and TF books.

2.12 We recommended that the Management direct the Inventory Committee to
conduct comprehensive physical count earlier to give enough time to prepare
the RPCPPE, so any discrepancy found in the accounting and property records
may be reconciled as soon as possible;
2.13 Further, require the Accountant and the Property Custodian to exert effort to
reconcile their respective records, dispose and drop the unserviceable
properties from the accounting records based on Inventory and Inspection
Report for Unserviceable Property and verify/trace the unreconciled balances
and prepare the necessary adjusting entries to correct any unrecorded/
erroneous recording of PPEs to come up with the correct balances of the
accounts.

3.7 We recommended that Management direct the Project Engineers concerned to
regularly supervise/monitor the implementation of projects to determine the
actual status at a certain period before the targeted completion date to
recommend measures to hasten completion. Carefully plan and implement
activities required for the timely implementation of the projects to ensure that
the intended benefits of the projects are promptly availed by the constituents of
the Municipality.

4.7 We recommended that Management stop the practice of using the LDRRMF
for disbursements that are not related to disaster risk reduction and
management and strictly conform with COA Circular No. 2012-002 and Item
4.3 of NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC JMC No. 2014-1.

5.5 We recommended that Management ensure that all changes in the annual
budget be made through the issuance of an ordinance and adhere strictly to
Section 336 of R.A. No. 7160 on the augmentation/realignment of funds in the
annual budget.

6.6 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare individual payslip
for each employee to serve as a personal record of their salaries earned and all
authorized deductions withheld and shall be furnished to each employee
monthly as required in Section 263 b.1 of the GAAM.

7.8 We recommended that the Management direct the concerned officials to pass
an Appropriation Ordinance providing specific allocation for the operation and
maintenance of the PRDP subproject and open a separate trust fund account
where the allocated fund will be deposited pursuant to Section 7.06 of the
Implementation Management Agreement and Item 2.8 of PRDP Intensified
Building-Up of Infrastructure and Logistics for Development (I-BUILD)
Component Operations Manual.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Ensure that all procurement must be meticulously and judiciously planned by the Procuring Entity. PCF must be used only for petty operating expenses and expenditures with huge amount must not be charged to PCF. Splitting of PCF transactions must also be strictly avoided;
Direct the Municipal Treasurer to:
Strictly comply with documentary requirements prescribed in COA Circular 2012-001, with regard to the forms and reports in the replenishment of PCF;
Refrain from successive replenishment of PCF when disbursements did not reach the 75 per cent threshold as well as excessive replenishment of fund beyond the monthly PCF budget; and
Consider also limiting the amount of PCF budget to a maximum amount of ₱100,000.00 and any expenses of same nature or type for the month exceeding the amount of ₱100,000.00 must be made thru public bidding.
Require the Municipal Accountant to ensure that the required documents are properly accomplished and attached in the PCV prior to granting the replenishment of Petty Cash Fund, and ensure that the petty cash expenses are properly classified and recorded in the books.

We recommended that Management:
Stop the practice of granting cash advance without legally authorized specific purpose and for payments on account of infrastructure projects or other undertaking on a project basis, and strictly abide by the provisions COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 and Section 89 of P.D. 1445;
Procurement of goods and infrastructure projects must be made through regular modes of procurement provided in RA No. 9184, and payment must be made directly to suppliers and not through CA; and
Granting of CA must be limited to those allowed/ provided in COA Circular No. 97-002, and there must be a sufficient and relevant documents establishing the basis in granting of cash advances as well as supporting computations in determining the reasonableness of CA.

We recommended that Management:
Create an Inventory Committee to conduct a complete physical count of PPE in accordance with COA Circular 2020-006 and ensure timely submission of RPCPPE to the Office of the Auditor;
Require the GSO and the Municipal Accountant to maintain an updated Property Cards and Property Ledger Cards, respectively, per category on all PPE as recorded in the books of accounts, and continuously coordinate with each other in order to reconcile the discrepancies in the respective PPE accounts; and
Management Comment:
Partial physical count of PPE (mainly consisting of high value PPE) was conducted by the Municipal Government. Management committed to conduct a complete physical count of PPEs to ensure the existence of PPE owned and possessed by the Municipal Government.
Because of the lack of staff in MACCO and General Services Office (MGSO), Property Cards and Property Ledger Cards were not religiously maintained. However, Management positively finds solutions to address this issue.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Give priority on the implementation of the LGSF-DRRAP pre-identified project. If the same is not feasible or practical, the LGU must judiciously and meticulously plan a new project provided that it is within the priority programs and projects provided in LBC No. 134; and
Require the Local Finance Committee to prepare the quarterly reports on fund utilization and status of program/project implementation using the format prescribed in Annex C of LBC No. 134, until such time that the Unified Reporting System of the LGU has been developed and can be used by the LGU.

We recommended that Management require the LSB:
Formulate a three-year program for strategic allocation of the SEF for the actual and practical needs of public schools’ bearing in mind the importance of the same in the preparation of the Annual SEF Budget;
Maximize the allocation and utilization of the SEF budget, ensure that various PPAs incorporated therein are substantial/adequate, practical and convenient to support the essential needs of the schools, especially the expenditures for health and sanitation which is much needed in this pandemic situation and the anticipated face-to-face classes of students in  school, and the PPAs’ allocated must be those allowable expenditures prescribed in the aforesaid DepEd, DBM & DILG Joint Circulars; and
Revert to GF the amount paid to risograph machine that should have been charged to SEF.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Adhere strictly to the provisions of DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2017-1, ensuring that items of expenditures appropriated and charged to 20 Percent Development Fund must be those expressly stated/ allowed on item 3 of such JMC;
Be cautious and judicious in the preparation of 20 Percent Development Fund Plans/Programs and ensure that the same only includes Priority Development Projects and not those expenditure items explicitly prohibited/ not allowed on Item 4 of such JMC; and
Require the Municipal Engineer or concerned LGU Official to prepare the quarterly reports on the utilization of the 20 Percent DF, as required in DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1 dated November 4, 2020.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Require the LDRRM Officer to strictly ensure that only those expenditures that are supportive or related to key objectives of DRRM Projects and Activities must be charged to Local DRRM Fund. Likewise, ensure that expenditure is in accordance with LDRRM Investment Plan or revise the Plan if the same includes admin expenses;
Provide a separate budget other than LDRRMF intended solely for the regular budget of LDRRM Office for those expenditures that are considered administrative in nature; and
Be cautious and meticulous in the preparation of LDRRM Investment Plan and ensure that the same does not include administrative expense and only those expenditures supportive to DRRM Projects and Activities or related to key objectives of disaster risk reduction and management must be included.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the concerned Municipal officials and employees to strictly comply with the mandatory submission of purchase orders and their supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor within five days after perfection for a timely and complete conduct of auditorial review.
We also invited the attention of Management to Section 4.0, the Penalty Clause, of COA Circular No. 2009-001, which states that the inability of the officials and employees concerned to comply with the requirements of the same must be subject to administrative disciplinary action and the transactions covered by the unsubmitted documents may be suspended in audit:

We recommended that Management through the GAD Focal Point System:
Require the MPDC to maintain GAD database with sex-disaggregated data and other relevant data that will serve as gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation, and effectively address gender issues;
Require the GAD focal person to prepare the GAD Agenda, especially this year with new three year term of leadership in office, to identify priority gender issues to be addressed by the LGU and
Require the concerned GAD Focal Person to submit the GAD Agenda and the DILG-received GAD AR and its supporting documents within the prescribed period to facilitate the evaluation of GAD Plan and Budget.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
a. Direct the Municipal Accountant to give preferential attention on the preparation
and submission of the required BRS, prepare SLs per bank accounts, review the
records relevant to the Cash in Bank account and effect necessary adjustments to
correct the amount presented in the financial statements and other documents;
and
b. Direct the Municipal Treasurer to update religiously the cash books and the data
reflected be preferably considered in the preparation of the BRS to ensure that
any reconciling items will be accordingly adjusted.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant make the appropriate
adjusting entry for the erroneous entry made in the Calendar Year (CY) 2020, and in
the succeeding years, ensure that journal entries relating to the PCF are properly
accounted for.
We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer ensure that the PCF is
fully settled at the end of each year and its unutilized balance is returned/refunded
and deposited to the AGDB. Strict adherence to Section 48 of the Manual on the
LGU-NGAS, particularly the full liquidation of the Petty Cash account at the end of
the year, is enjoined.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
a. Enforce the immediate liquidation/settlement of long outstanding cash advances,
otherwise, require the immediate withholding of salary of those officers and
employees who were unable to comply with the requirement;
b. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to strengthen the internal control on the grant
and liquidation of cash advances by complying with the provisions under Section
Nos. 4 and 5 of the COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997;
c. Require the Municipal Accountant to closely monitor the liquidation of
outstanding cash advances and send written demand on the submission of
liquidation report within the reglementary period;
d. Stop the practice of granting additional cash advances to those who have
unliquidated cash advances and take legal actions against former employees, who
are no longer in the government service, with unliquidated cash advances; and
e. Verify the collectability of unliquidated cash advances of more than 10 years,
otherwise, request for authority to write-off dormant accounts guided by the
conditions and procedures prescribed under COA Circular No. 2016-005.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
a. Direct the Municipal Accountant and the GSO to strictly adhere to the provisions
of Sections 114 and 121 of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs on the proper
recording and reporting of transactions, which requires that all regular purchases
of supplies must be booked through the inventory account and the SSMI is needed
as a basis for recording the expenditures using appropriate expenditure accounts;
and
b. Direct the Municipal Accountant and the GSO to maintain the SLC and stock
cards for each supplies and materials, to monitor and control the status of the
stocks on hand.

We recommended that Management:
a. Make utmost effort to achieve a more in depth, complete physical count of all
PPEs of the Municipality and submit the same within the prescribed period;
b. Require the GSO and the Municipal Accountant to maintain an updated Property
Cards and Property Ledger Cards, respectively, per category on all PPE as
recorded in the books of accounts, ensure the maintenance of the same for the
recent years’ procurements and continuously work on the previous acquisitions;
and
c. Require both offices to reconcile property records with the accounting records
and adjust the books of accounts and the property records, as warranted, after
reconciliation.

We recommended that Management comply with the procedural guidelines as
stated under the Joint Circular as follows:
a. Review and analyze all recorded payable accounts that remained outstanding for
two years or more; and
b. Prepare the corresponding Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) and revert to
Government Equity all A/Ps that remained outstanding for two years duly
supported by a List of A/Ps reverted.

In addition, SLs must be maintained for all A/Ps reverted to Government
Equity for monitoring purposes and for future reference in case payments will be/had
been made.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal
Accountant to analyze the affected accounts to determine to which funds the amounts
are due and prepare necessary adjusting journal entries to reflect the correct
balances of each account in their respective fund classification.
Also, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant conduct periodic
reconciliation/analysis, preferably on a quarterly basis, of the reciprocal accounts
“Due from/Due to Other Funds” to ensure that necessary adjustments in the books of
accounts are made on time.

We recommended that Management:
a. Require the LSB to formulate a three-year program for strategic allocation of the
SEF for public schools’ needs;
b. Give preferential attention to the allocation and implementation of PPAs that
could help support public schools in their operations and maintenance through
the Annual SEF Budget;
c. Through the LSB, utilize the SEF Budget efficiently and effectively by prioritizing
programs and activities that would help improve the operations of public schools
for the benefit of the teachers, students and other stakeholders; and
d. Strictly observe the provisions of DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s.
2017 in the utilization of the SEF for its intended purpose.

We recommended that Management ensure that the cash donations are
properly taken up and recorded in the TF books in its appropriate account. Also, we
recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare adjusting journal entries for
the erroneous entry for recording.

We recommended that Management:
a. Utilize the unexpended LDDRMF for PPAs that would help mitigate and reduce
the impact and/or effects of calamities and adversities that may befall the
Municipality;
b. Direct the attention of the Municipal Engineer to evaluate the true cause/s of the
discontinuance/suspension of the construction during the previous administration,
and take immediate and appropriate actions;
c. Compel the contractor to resume the operations and complete the projects; and
d. For the succeeding LDRRM projects, take appropriate action, legal or otherwise,
to compel the contractor to complete the projects in due time.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
a. Instruct the LDRRM Officer to promptly submit the monthly Report on the
Sources and Utilization of LDRRMF on or before the 15th day after the end of
each month pursuant to the procedures of the aforecited Circular, to facilitate
verification thereof;
b. Comply with the monthly Report on the Sources and Utilization of LDRRMF for
the CY 2020 that were not submitted; and
c. Take appropriate actions in facilitating and monitoring the preparation and
submission of the required monthly reports for the timely evaluation and
validations of the disbursements from the fund.

We recommended that Management establish and maintain GAD Database to
provide accurate information and bases for an effective gender planning,
programming and policy formulation, pursuant to Section 4.1 (B) of the PCW, DILG,
DBM, and NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
a. Require the LGU officials and employees to submit promptly their claims
together with complete relevant supporting documents to the Office of the
Municipal Accountant;
b. Hire additional competent staffs and personnel in the Accounting Office to
accommodate the voluminous transactions occurring each month in the
Municipality, and to hasten the process of submission of the necessary accounts to
the Office of the Auditor;
c. Evaluate the process of preparing the disbursement vouchers, RCIs, RCDs,
RAAFs, and relevant documents to prevent further occurrence of this deficiency
in the succeeding years and be reminded that processing of vouchers must only be
undertaken when they were properly approved and with complete
documentation; and
d. Remind the concerned officials and employees of the administrative or criminal
liability, under Section 39 (1) (3) of PD No. 1445 and Article 218 of the Revised
Penal Code, as amended by Section 41 of Republic Act No. 10951, which may be
imposed as a consequence of their inability or refusal to render accounts without
justifiable cause.

We recommended that Management:
a. Compel the contractor to repair the existing defects and deficiencies of the project
by removing and replacing the concrete portion of the project in compliance with
the Order of PRDP Inspectorate Team;
b. Show cause why no liquidated damages were impose to the contractor for the
delayed implementation of the project as required in Section 68 of the Revised
Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR) of Republic Act (RA) 9184;
c. For succeeding PRDP Projects, take appropriate action, legal or otherwise, to
compel the contractor to complete the project in due time; and
d. Promptly organize the PRDP MPMIU before or at an earlier stage of the project
implementation in order to effectively and efficiently manage the implementation
of PRDP subproject so as to minimize or avoid delay in its implementation/
completion.

We recommended that the present administration and the concerned
Chairman and Members of the BAC strictly adhere to the rules and regulations
provided in RA 9184 and its revised IRR, especially in the revision of ABC wherein
the increase must not be more than 20 percent of the ABC for the last failed bidding.
The I-REAP Focal Person, Proponent Group Representative, and other
concerned personnel who prepare the I-REAP Procurement Plan must conduct a
thorough study of the report. Ensure that the procurement package to be
incorporated in the I-REAP procurement plan is available in the market and its price
is updated/realistic and within the estimated budget, in order to avoid failure of
bidding and revision of Approved Budget of Contract.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
a. Take appropriate and immediate action to warrant full operation of the Charcoal
Briquette Enterprise especially in the reorganization of personnel to manage the
enterprise;
b. Ensure that the new Personnel Organization is competent to manage and operate
the enterprise to ensure its operation and sustainability of income generating
livelihood to its intended beneficiaries;
c. Conduct training programs to members/ personnel involve in Charcoal Briquette
production, if needed, in order to improve its operation instead of purchasing
various machineries and equipment which may not be currently used in
operation; and
d. Furnish the COA Auditor with I-REAP Procurement Plan to verify the validity of
procurement.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
a. Direct the Municipal Accountant to revert the entry made to transfer the PPE
account from the PRDP TF to the GF books;
b. Require the I-REAP focal person/s to submit the complete documents relevant to
the project to the Office of the Auditor; and
c. Instruct the concerned officers to revisit and strictly follow the provisions of the
PRDP Manual, specifically the I-REAP component, to ensure its compliance at all
times.

We recommended that Management adhere strictly to the prescribed
financing mix of LGU Counterpart for PRDP subprojects, especially I-REAP
subproject, as provided in the PRDP Financial Management Operations Guidelines.
The LGU BAC must thoroughly review the Procurement Plan of PRDP- I-
REAP subproject and ensure that the total cost of procurement activities is within the
prescribed financing mix threshold as provided in PRDP Financial Management
Operation Guidelines.
Refrain from procuring goods if its cost already exceeded the financing mix
threshold particularly if its amount is exorbitantly higher than those provided in the
guidelines.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
a. Strictly observe the timeline for submission of Financial Reports and Financial
Statements in accordance with the existing rules and regulations;
b. Submit the financial statements and other accounting reports that are already due
for submission; and
c. Identify and resolve the causes of delay and non-submission of the mandatory
financial statements and project reports with complete supporting
documents/schedules within the prescribed period, otherwise, institute
appropriate sanctions against the responsible officers for the continued non-
compliance with existing regulations.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
a. Adhere strictly to the pertinent laws, rules and regulations with regard to current
procurement of the LGU. In case of Emergency Procurement under the
Bayanihan Act, the LGU must comply with the latest procedure and
documentation required in RA No. 11469, GPPB Circular No. 1-2020 and COA
Circular 2012-001;
b. Direct the Accountant to strengthen its internal control in the pre-audit of
financial transactions. Ensure that the supporting documents are complete and
properly accomplished/filled out as to details and necessary information;
otherwise, refrain from processing the claim or payment of the same;
c. Be mindful/cautious in making journal entry in the books, ensure that
procurement of machineries and equipment must be capitalized as asset in the
books and not charge to repairs and maintenance expense;
d. Make the necessary adjusting entry for those Machineries and Equipment
erroneously charged to expense account in order to reflect the correct amount of
its respective PPE accounts; and
e. Submit the lacking documents and rectify the deficiencies of the aforementioned
financial transactions in order to avoid suspension/ and or disallowance in audit.

We recommended that the LGUs of the province further enhance the quality of its
individual LDRRM management processes by taking concrete actions to address the issues
related to manpower, data and information, and formalization to reinforce the
establishment of an enabling level of coordination in a collective setting and ensure disaster
resiliency.

In view of the pointed keynote areas for improvement, we recommended the
following advancement measures:

a. Address the identified gap in the budgeting phase by conducting analysis on the
current workloads through unloading and/or fair distribution of the tasks of the
competent and technical people, who will prepare the POWs and plans and be
made available during budget deliberations;

b. Gaps/delays in the implementation phase be addressed by making constant
follow-ups with the input providers-suppliers for the immediate delivery of
construction materials. Otherwise, legal remedies are resorted to, if warranted;

c. Enhance the delivery of DF initiatives by making sure that the most prioritized
developmental PPAs whose results and outcomes address the most needed
interventions by the community, in general; and

d. Establish a feedback assessment mechanism to follow through the outcomes and
impacts of the delivered DF initiatives to the end-users vis-à-vis with the
identified baseline indicators/targets/objectives set beforehand.

In view of the pointed keynote areas for improvement, we recommended the
following advancement measures:

a. Address the identified gap/causes in the non-compliances of the required
supporting documents to all government disbursements, otherwise there will be
issuances of Notice of Suspension, when warranted;

b. Gaps/delays in the implementation phase be addressed by making constant
follow-ups with the input providers-suppliers for the immediate and full delivery
of approved PPAs. Otherwise, legal remedies are resorted to, if warranted;

c. Enhance the delivery of LDRRM initiatives by making sure that the most
prioritized LDRRM PPAs whose results and outcomes address the most needed
interventions by the community, in general; and

d. Establish a feedback assessment mechanism tool to follow through the outcomes
and impacts of the delivered LDRRM initiatives to the end-users vis-à-vis with
the identified baseline indicators/targets/objectives set beforehand.

With the areas of improvement noted, the following advancement measures are co-
developed with management:

a. Capacitate and enhance the General Services Office (GSO) by taking concrete
actions to address issues related to manpower and formalization of the office to
ensure effective and efficient management of all PPE owned by the LGU;

b. The Local Chief Executive shall capacitate and support its Inventory
Committee in conducting the complete physical count of all the agency’s
Property, Plant and Equipment taking into consideration the standard and
guidelines provided in the COA Circular 2020-006 in order to reconcile the
PPE records and eventually arrive at the correct balances of the PPE Accounts;

c. The Municipal Accounting Office shall capacitate and analyze the current
workloads of the manpower, and delegate the task on the establishment and
maintenance and update of PPE Ledger Cards for reporting purposes; and the
Supply Officer to set-up and maintain Property Cards for an efficient
monitoring and reconciliations of the agency’s PPE with that of the accounting;

3. We reiterated our recommendation that all the AOs concerned be required to liquidate their cash advances on time so that proper accounting could be made thereof to ensure fairness of accounts presented on the financial statements.
4. The Municipal Accountant in her capacity as the internal auditor must strictly implement the rules and regulations on cash advances to avoid the accumulation of huge amount of unliquidated cash advances.

We recommend the immediate transfer of the unexpended balance of the QRF and LDRRMF-MOOE to a Special Trust Fund Account pursuant to the provisions of Republic Act (RA) No. 1021 and COA Circular 2012-002 to ensure that the unspent balances are readily available for relief and recovery projects and activities.

9. We reiterated our recommendation that Management warrant the timely submission of documents as required by the memorandum circular as to ensure the proper and effective implementation of the programs charged under LCPC.

10. We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipality submit a duly approved 10-year SWMP in order to properly address the need to ensure sustainability, viability, effectiveness and relevance in relation to local and international developments in the field of solid waste management.

11. We recommended that the GFPS fast-track the establishment of a GAD database to provide accurate information and bases for an effective gender planning, programming and policy formulation pursuant to Section 4.1.B.1 of JMC No.2013-01 of the PCW,DILG,DBM and NEDA.
12. We recommended that the LGU formulate rules and regulations which will serve as their own criteria for the implementation of the AICS.

13. We recommended that the management direct the MPDC to prepare and submit on time the above-mentioned report to the Office of the Auditor for evaluation on the agency's utilization of the said Development Fund.

14. We reiterated our recommendation for Senior Management to take the appropriate action, legal or otherwise, to compel the contractor to complete the project.
15. Organize the Municipal Project Management and Implementation Unit (MPMIU) concerned, through the issuance of written order to allow them to function accordingly.

17. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct all department heads concerned to exert coordinated efforts in order to come up with an updated PPE.
18. We further recommend that all Local Government Unit (LGU) officials/employees having direct concern over these unbooked properties be directed to retrieve the necessary documents and submit the same to the Property Officer and Accounting Unit for proper recording/accounting in the Agency's books and that similar transactions in the future be duly accounted for upon acceptance.
19. Likewise, apply the necessary depreciation costs where warranted. And, the request for inspection over the unserviceable properties be immediately prepared for proper action.

20. We recommended that all accountable officers concerned be required to liquidate their cash advances on time so that proper accounting could be made thereof to ensure fairness of accounts presentation on the financial statements.
21. The Municipal Accountant, in her capacity as the internal auditor, must strictly implement the rules and regulations on cash advances to avoid the accumulation of huge amount of unliquidated cash advances.
22. Lastly refrain from granting cash advances to accountable officers, officials and employees unless the previous ones are fully settled.

23. We recommend that all concerned be required to observe full compliance to the aforecited rules and regulations to warrant timely rectification of defects and thereby reducing the risks of government funds wastage.

24. We recommend that the Management warrant the timely submission of documents as required by the Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120, so as to ensure the proper and effective implementation of the programs charged under the Local Council for the Protection of Children (LCPC).

29. We recommend that the Management warrant the timely submission of documents as required by the Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120, so as to ensure the proper and effective implementation of the programs charged under the Local Council for the Protection of Children (LCPC).

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct all department heads concerned to exert coordinated efforts in order to come up with an updated RPCPPE.
We further recommended that all LGU officials/employees having direct concerns over these unbooked  properties be directed to retrieve the necessary documents and submit the same to the Property Officer and Accounting Unit for proper recording/accounting in the Agency’s books and that similar transactions in the future be duly accounted for upon acceptance.
Likewise, apply the necessary depreciation costs where warranted.

We reiterated our recommendation that all the AOs concerned be required to liquidate their cash advances on time so that proper accounting could be made thereof to ensure fairness of accounts presentation on the financial statements.
The Municipal Accountant in her capacity as the internal auditor must strictly implement the rules and regulations on cash advances to avoid the accumulation of huge amount of unliquidated cash advances.
Lastly, refrain from granting cash advances to AOs, officials and employees unless the previous ones are fully settled.

We recommended that the documents regarding the Dispersal Program be submitted by the Municipal Agricultural Officer to the Municipal Accountant for proper accounting.
Copies of the same must likewise be submitted to the Commission on Audit.

We recommended the immediate transfer of the unexpended balance of the QRF and LDRRMF-MOOE to a Special Trust Fund Account pursuant to the provisions of Republic Act (RA) No. 10121 and COA Circular 2012-002 to ensure that the unspent balances are readily available for relief and recovery projects and activities.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management warrant the timely submission of documents as required by the memorandum circular so as to ensure the proper and effective implementation of the programs charged under the LCPC.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipality submit a duly approved 10-year SWMP in order to properly address the need to ensure sustainability, viability, effectiveness and relevance in relation to local and international developments in the field of solid waste management.

We recommended that the GFPS fast-track the establishment of a GAD database to provide accurate information and bases for an effective gender planning, programming and policy formulation pursuant to Section 4.1.B.1 of JMC No. 2013-01 of the PCW, DILG, DBM and NEDA.
We recommended that the LGU formulate rules and regulations which will serve as their own criteria for the Implementation of the AICS.

We recommended that Management direct the MPDC to prepare and submit on time the above-mentioned report to the Office of the Auditor for evaluation on the agency’s utilization of the said Development Fund.

We reiterated our recommendation for Senior Management to take the appropriate action, legal or otherwise, to compel the contractor to complete the project.
Organize the Municipal Project Management and Implementation Unit (MPMIU) concerned, through the issuance of a written order to allow them to function accordingly.

We recommended that Senior Management take appropriate and immediate measures to warrant full operation of the Enterprise. And, also organize the Rural Improvement Club which was identified to manage business operation of the Charcoal Briquette Production.

a) Address the causes/gaps in the non-preparation and non-submission of the
required Monthly Bank Reconciliations Statements;
b) Revisit workload plan among the personnel in the Municipal Accounting Office
by ensuring fair and balance workloads and elimination of work redundancies
among them, to comply with preparation and submission of the required reports;
c) Address all the causes of non-liquidations by all accountable employees and
officers. Request for write-off of the accounts proven impossible of collectability.
Cause analysis and reconciliations of records and issuance of demand letters to
concerned officials and employees with outstanding cash advances;
d) Establish additional controls by opening and maintaining a separate bank account
for 5% Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund for accountability and
reporting purposes;
e) Institute additional internal control measures over the grant of cash advances for
officers and employees by discontinuing the grant of additional advances to those
with unliquidated ones;
f) Create Inventory Committees to timely and strategically plan the conduct of
complete physical count of PPE and prepare/submit the corresponding RCPPE and
IRRUP and reconciles the results of the count with the GSO and accounting
records;
g) Exhaust all efforts to locate relevant data/information pertaining to the donated and
disposed PPE as basis in the book recording;
h) Maintain the PPELCs and PCs to facilitate reconciliation and fair presentation of
the PPE accounts in the books;
i) Exhaust all efforts to locate relevant data/ documents pertaining to the
unverified/unconfirmed loans payable and due to NGAs balances in the books of
accounts before resorting to a request for an authority to write off dormant fund
transfer provided in COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016; and
j) Take up appropriate Reclassifying/Adjusting Journal Entries (RAJE/PAJEs) to
fairly present the affected account balances in the financial statements.

We recommended that the LGUs of the province collectively and individually
ensure the effective and efficient delivery of LDRRM responsibilities by enhancing the level
of coordination through managing the challenges on the dependencies that affect the
essential management processes.

We recommended that the LGUs of the province ensure the effective and efficient
execution of disaster responses through the establishment of appropriately contextualized
disaster protocols.

7.    We recommended and Management  agreed  to  direct  the  Municipal

5.     We  recommended  and  Management  agreed  that   the   Municipal

       support the validity of claims and correctness of the amount of cash

11.  We  reiterated  our  recommendation  that  Management  direct   the

       all PPE from TF to GF and compute the corresponding depreciation.

6.    We recommended that the Accountant:


        a. cause the reconciliation of the balance of Due from LGUs account
           with the records of the Barangay by instructing the  Bookkeeper,
           with the assistance of the  Barangay  Treasurers  to  trace  the
           actual utilization of the funds downloaded with reference to the
           schedule in Annex A in order to determine whether the funds were
           utilized for its intended purpose;



        b. Subsequently, require the concerned Barangays to  photocopy  all
           transaction documents related to the utilization of funds and to
           submit the same to Accounting as basis for the liquidation;



        c. Instruct the Bookkeeper to record back the unutilized  funds  in
           the books of the Barangay by crediting the Due to  LGUs  account
           and debit Prior Years’ Adjustments to establish the  balance  of
           the liability, if any and  inform  the  concerned  Barangays  to
           return the unused funds; and



        d. Reconcile regularly the balance of Due from LGUs  account  under
           the LGU’s books and Due to LGUs account  in  the  books  of  the
           Barangay since the latter is maintained in the Accounting Office
           to monitor the utilization of the fund.

6.    We recommended that the  Municipal  Accountant  exert  effort  to
       trace the cause of the variance of these intra-agency  accounts  and
       effect the necessary adjustments through Journal  Entry  Voucher  to
       reflect  a  correct  or  equal  amount/balance  in   the   financial
       statements.

6.   We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed  to  trace  the
       details of these accounts and to effect the necessary adjustments in
       the books for any erroneous entries. We further recommend  that  the
       Accounting Unit maintain a subsidiary ledger for these  account  for
       monitoring purposes and to support  the  balance  in  the  financial
       statements.

5.    We recommended that the Municipal Accountant properly disclose in
       the Notes to  Financial  Statements  not  only  the  utilization  of
       current  appropriation  of  LDRRMF  but  also  the  details  of  the
       previous’ years unexpended LDRRMF as required in Section  5.1.16  of
       COA Circular No. 2012-02.

6.    We recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit his basis  or
       valid supporting documents for the adjustment made  on  Due  to  BIR
       account  amounting  to  Php1,850,404.80.  Likewise,  SL  should   be
       maintained by the Accounting Office for this account for  monitoring
       purposes and to support  the  balance  reflected  in  the  financial
       statement.  Henceforth,  all  adjusting  entries  made   should   be
       supported with valid documents for evaluation.

6. We recommended that the Management require the supplier to submit  a
       warranty security in the form of surety  bond  or  require  them  to
       refund  the  amount  equivalent  to  1   percent   retention   money
       considering that the one year warranty period has  not  yet  lapsed.
       Henceforth, the concerned officials be enjoined to strictly  observe
       the requirement on posting of warranty  securities  as  provided  in
       Section 62.1 of the IRR of RA 9184.

2.  We  recommended  that   Management   discontinue   the   grant   of
       Transportation Allowance to all officials who were issued with motor
       vehicles. Ensure compliance with the provision of LBC No. 103, dated
       May 15, 2013 on the  grant  of  Transportation  Allowance  to  avoid
       disallowance in audit.

7. We recommended and  Management  agrees  to  require   the  concerned
       officer to prepare a report  of  unpaid  rental  market  stalls  and
       submit to the Accounting  Office  for  recording  to  recognize  all
       collectibles and earned income in the  financial  statements  as  at
       given period.

6. We recommended that the LGU use the 2% of  the  Php5,000.00  maximum
       monthly compensation limit as reference in  computing  the  employer
       share for Pag-IBIG Contribution as provided in RA 9679 or a  maximum
       of P100.00 regardless of the amount that the employee contributes
       for the personal share.

6. We recommended that the LGU strictly observe the  provision  of  BIR
       Revenue Regulation No. 11-2018 and Revenue Memorandum Order  No.  8-
       2003 to  ensure  prompt  and  full  remittance  of  taxes  withheld.
       Penalties imposed on  late  remittance  should  be  charged  to  the
       concerned personnel who failed to remit the taxes on time.

2. We recommended that the unutilized balance of the funds of completed
       projects be immediately  returned  to  the  source  agency  and  the
       implementing office to continue to monitor the implementation of the
       remaining project funded  under  the  Assistance  to  Municipalities
       program.

3. We recommended that the implementing office continue to monitor  the
       implementation of all on-going development projects  to  ensure  its
       completion within the  established  time  frame,  thereby  providing
       immediate economic benefits to the intended beneficiaries. Also, the
       Management is advised to fast track the procurement of the remaining
       unimplemented projects and to realign  the  unused  funds  to  other
       development projects.

Subject the transferred property to depreciation; - Update ARE every three years after issuance to ensure that property is still under the accountability of the accountable officer to whom property was issued; - Issue Inventory Custodian Slip on properties considered as small tangible items pursuant to COA Circular 2005-02 (Annex C) in coordination with COA Circular No. 97-005 (Annex D); - Maintain and update the PPELC as well as the different property ledgers; - Maintain and update property cards by the Property Custodian; - Reconcile the PPELC, property cards and the physically counted properties; On properties not provided with depreciation: - Exert all efforts to secure the documents of properties not provided with depreciation; - Appraise all properties not provided with depreciation and make a sound decision on the amounts of impairments to present fairly the balances of the PPE and then provide depreciation; On trust fund properties: - Exert effort to locate documents related to the booked-up under Trust Fund; - Secure documents related to the PPEs recorded in the Trust Fund books, if none can be retrieved from the file of the Municipality, by communicating with the funding agency; and - Transfer all completed projects’ properties to General Fund and subject the same to depreciation.

observations.recommendation: Therefore, we recommended the following to ensure that Cash in Bank balance is fairly presented in the Financial Statements: • Exert effort to trace the documents for the unrecorded bank accounts and include the same in the Cash in Bank balance; • Refer to the granting agency if no liquidation report is necessary to close the grant; • If remaining amount will accrue to the benefit of the Municipality, revert balances of the account to the General Fund to close the existing accounts; • If no liquidation report is available for certain dormant accounts, enroll in online banks or request for bank snapshot.
observations.reason: Two (2) of these unrecorded bank accounts were with “dormant” status totaling P4,262,942.18 per depository bank’s confirmation which is not advantageous to the government as this will incur penalty charges and the sum of money kept in a long period of time hinders the government to have applied it to a more beneficial project.

observations.recommendation: Therefore, we recommended that the Municipal Treasurer, in as much as the accountable forms are under her responsibility to: • Demand the presentation/liquidation/return of the subject accountable forms from responsible personnel; • Stop any releases of accountable forms to accountable officers who were with unreturned/unliquidated forms.
