observations.recommendation: Therefore, we recommended the following: • Update the cashbook based on the Report of Collections and Deposits submitted by Liquidating Officer on a daily basis; • Close the cashbooks at the end of each month by double-ruling below the last entries in the cashbook, showing further the running balances; • Certify all cashbooks at the end of each quarter; • Record the COH on a monthly basis collections made for SEF and Trust Funds; • At least once per quarter, pursuant to the above ruling, the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant conduct reconciliation of their books and to reflect all reconciling items in both books affected to accurately present the Municipality on the balances of the Cash in Bank, Cash Local Treasury and Payroll accounts as presented in the Financial Statement.
observations.reason: MTO is already complying with the audit recommend ations.

Therefore, we recommended the following to the Municipal Accountant and the GSO Officer to ensure correct balances of the inventory account in the financial statements: • Employ the Perpetual Inventory Method; • Maintain the SLC and the stock cards upon delivery of purchased inventories; • Use the Moving Average Method for costing inventories in conformity with existing regulations upon the application of the Perpetual Inventory Method.

observations.recommendation: Therefore, we recommended the following: • Require the submission of the completed vouchers of expenses that needs to be accrued at the end of the year from different offices concerned; • Record the accrual of expenses in the books of the accountant in the period of its occurrence following the accrual accounting principle.
observations.reason: Still to adjust the prior year’s expenses not recorded as expenses for the current year.

observations.recommendation: Therefore, the audit team recommended the following: a. Surcharges should not be paid out of the government funds but by the person responsible of causing the delay in the payment of utility bills. On that note, he is reminded to pay the bills on or before the due date to avoid penalties and surcharges. b. Stop charging unrelated expenditures to SEF pursuant to Section 272 of the Local Government Code. Instead, the local school board should proposed programs and projects to address the emerging issues on the delivery of basic education. c. Payments by the government should be done in the usual process and not by reimbursement. In this way, withholding the right taxes due the government is possible. d. Strict adherence to Procurement Law is a must in all disbursements especially the prohibition of branding in government procurements.
observations.reason: Mgt. is still complying with the audit recommendations.

observations.recommendation: Therefore, we recommended the following in future : • Write as payee the destination account number and not the name of the Municipal Treasurer; and • Maintain cashbooks for each PRDP accounts for easy verification of fund transactions.

Therefore, we recommended the following: a. Initiate a move to follow-up the approval of the Municipality’s 10-year plan by the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board in order to finalize and implement the programs stated therein or seek for authorization to put into operation the programs; b. Convert the open dumpsite into a controlled facility. Fast track the establishment of sanitary landfill and operate the material recovery facility. Also, designate personnel that should focus on the operation of the controlled dumpsite; and c. Implement the planned program/projects to address present environmental concerns of the municipality.

Therefore, we recommended that the Municipal Treasurer will maintain cashbooks for each project under the Trust Fund whether or not a separate bank account for the project is maintained pursuant to COA Circular No. 94-013.

Therefore, we recommended that management enjoin the Municipal accountant to strictly comply with the rules and regulations on the submission of paid disbursement vouchers including supporting documents and other Financial Reports. Although, not all of the beneficiaries have been successful in the introduction of the DILEEP program, we still commend the efforts of the focal persons in implementing the program due to its undeniable impact on the lives of some marginalized beneficiaries who were granted the KABUHAYAN program. To ensure continued success of the program in future undertaking, since there are still rooms for improvement, we recommended the following: a. The municipality should institute some interventions like skills development, business and financial management and attitude development towards earnings/business. Also, develop a reward system for those successful beneficiaries. b. Strengthen the procurement process by strict adherence to the Procurement Law and indicate the specification of equipment and tools procured. An inspection of the procured items is a must before their delivery to the beneficiaries.

observations.recommendation: Therefore, we reiterated our last year’s recommendation: • Conduct an immediate and thorough physical count of PPE by designating personnel, who is not handling cash transactions, to do the activity. Subject the transferred property to depreciation; • Maintain and update the PPELC as well as the different property ledgers; • Maintain and update property cards by the Property Custodian; • Reconcile the PPELC, property cards and the physically counted properties; • If budget permits, create a General Services Office (GSO) manned by competent permanent personnel who will handle the Municipality’s properties. • Further, we recommended that the Municipal properties identified above that were not included in the conversion from NGAS to PPSAS be included to present fairly the PPE accounts in the financial statements.

observations.recommendation: Therefore, we recommended to the Management: • Change the scheme of the remittance of the collection of collectors to the Liquidating Officer in accordance to Section 29 of NGAS Volume 1, that is, to remit to Liquidating Officer all collections made during the day at the close of each business day for those situated near the Municipality’s Office and at least once a week or as soon as the collections reach P5,000.00 for those Collectors whose official station will take more than one day to travel; • No additional stub will be issued to the collector unless the former/remaining stubs on collector’s hand were already liquidated by him/her to the liquidating officer; • Prepare a pre-formatted Report of Collections and Deposits (RCD) to be filled-up manually by the collectors duly signed by them to ensure prompt remittance of collections.

Therefore, we reiterated our last year that management do the following: • Exert effort to demand immediately the liquidation of all outstanding cash advances from the concerned officials/employees pursuant to COA Circulars 97-002 and 2012-004. • Impose sanctions prescribed in the said Circulars for non-compliance thereof, such as withholding the salaries and other emoluments of the officials/employees who continuously disregard, fail or decline to comply with the demand, including filing appropriate charges against them. • Send demand letters to officials and employees who are no longer connected with the Municipality but whose whereabouts are still known to liquidate their cash advances and consider taking legal actions/remedy against them. • Gather all documents that will prove that liquidation of the named individuals were no longer possible such as those who are already deceased (e.g. death certificate) and whose last address was not known (e.g. retirement certification, clearances, etc.) and request for the write off of the receivable for fair presentation of the account in the Financial Statements taking into consideration the liability of those individuals who granted the clearances.

observations.recommendation: Therefore, in view of the above deficiencies/gaps noted in the procurement activities of the Municipality, we recommended the following: a. Strict adherence to the provisions of RA 9184 and the Procurement Manual of the Local Government Unit in future procurement undertakings, such as but not limited to the following: • Provide the necessary/information in the posted Philgeps purchases; • Utilize the use of the Philippines Bidding Documents; • No references to brand names by being specific on the specifications present only to a particular product instead make a general specification; • Follow procedures before resorting to negotiated procurements; • Do not employ “kaliwaan system”, and • Equip concerned officials especially the BAC with proper knowledge in carrying out functions, duties and responsibilities by attending series of seminars and trainings on RA 9184.

Therefore, we recommended the following: • As a withholding agent of the Government, the management must compute, deduct and remit the exact withholding tax pursuant to the January 1, 2009 table to conform with the Tax Reform Act of 1997 and to ensure that employees will not be burdened by paying high amounts of taxes that need to be paid in lump sum in year end; • Deduct and remit PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG contributions as required by the IRR of RA 7875 and RA 9679, respectively; • Follow the order of priority in deducting the mandatory and allowable deductions to the salaries of the employees; and • Preparation of payroll must be prepared by the Accounting Unit of the Municipality.

Therefore, we recommended the following: • As a withholding agent of the Government, the management must compute, deduct and remit the exact withholding tax; • Deduct and remit PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG contributions as required by the IRR of RA 7875 and RA 9679, respectively; • Follow the order of priority in deducting the mandatory and allowable deductions to the salaries of the employees; and • Preparation of payroll must be prepared by the Accounting Unit of the Municipality.

The management should also closely monitor the utilization and implementation of priority projects to maximize the usage of the fund. We recommended that Management require both the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to promptly cause the timely remittance of liabilities due to the national and corporate government agencies in order not to compromise the grant of benefits to the LGU’s officials and staff and at the same time avoid penalties/surcharges arising from late payments and to warrant timely use of these resources by the government Agencies concerned.

We recommended that the Management warrant the timely submission of documents as required by the memorandum circular so as to ensure the proper and effective implementation of the programs charged under the LCPC.

We recommended that the Municipality submit a duly approved 10-year Solid Waste Management Plan in order to properly address the need to ensure sustainability, viability, effectiveness and relevance in relation to local and international developments in the field of solid waste management.
We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Treasurer to apply for renewal to those officers whose fidelity bonds have already expired and monitor the said bonds regarding its expiration.

We reiterated our recommendation for the Senior Management to take the appropriate action, legal or otherwise, to compel the contractor to complete the project.
Liquidated damages may be demanded from them in case of negative slippage.

We recommended that the senior management take appropriate and immediate measures to warrant full operation of the Enterprise.
Continuing training programs relative to the Charcoal Briquette Production (and Marketing) may be availed through coordination with other national government agencies.
We recommended that Management, initiate positive steps towards generation of increased income from local sources to warrant delivery of additional developmental projects in the locality.

With the observations noted in audit, we recommended the following:

a. Address all the causes of the deficiencies noted in audit through institution of
internal control system on the usage and utilization of gasoline, oils and
lubricants;
b. Serially number the Driver’s Trip Tickets, and direct the concerned driver of
every service vehicle to accomplish properly and completely the form for every trip
made; and
c. Require the Municipal Treasurer and/ or the designated General Services Officer,
to submit within ten (10) days of the succeeding month, to the Office of the Auditor,
the Monthly Report of Fuel Consumptions together with the approved Monthly
Report of Official Travels by the Driver(s) in pursuant to the provisions of Section
361 of GAAM, Volume I and COA Circular No. 77-61 dated September 26, 1977.

With the observations noted in audit, we recommended the following:
1. Ensure that the PAPs funded out in the approved GAD Plan and Budget (GPB) are
implemented within the year as specified in the AIP. Exert extra efforts to fast
track the completion of those PAPs which remain unimplemented and those on-
going;
2. Endeavor to attend further GAD trainings and workshops to enhance skill and
knowledge on the development and utilization of the GAD tools; and
3. Strict practice of close monitoring and consistent evaluation of the
implementation of the PAPs identified in the AIP.

Thus, we recommended the following measures:

a. The Municipal Treasurer shall see to it that a staff is assigned to regularly
attend to the sorting and submission of the disbursement vouchers and
preparation of various financial reports to the Accounting Office within ten
days after the end of each month pursuant to pertinent rules and
regulations; and
b. The Accountant shall ensure submission of financial reports together with
the corresponding official receipts and validated deposits slips, as well as
disbursement vouchers/payrolls and their supporting documents pursuant
to Section 39 (1), (3) of Presidential Decree 1445 and COA Circular No.
2009-006 to the Office of the Auditor.

With these deficiencies noted in audit, we recommended the following:

a) Conduct a complete physical count of all assets owned by the Municipality;
b) Ensure that payments for every purchased property/equipment shall be made
only when there is evidence that same has been duly recorded in the PPELC the
acquisitions, additions and dispositions of the PPEs by the Property Custodian
and/ or Municipal Accountant;
c) Exert extra efforts to secure the documents of properties not provided with
depreciation;
d) Identify unserviceable properties and record the same in the Inventory and
Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property to facilitate the disposal and
eventual dropping from the books; and
e) Reconcile the PPE accounts to arrive at the correct balances of the PPEs per item.

To improve performance under the 20% DF, we recommended the following
measures:

a. Review and analyze the causes of the low and non-implementation of the 20% DF
projects, programs and activities, and adopt effective strategies to address them,
ensuring that the development objectives for which the fund has been created are
completely carried out and implemented for the long-term benefits of the
constituents;
b. Strictly adhere to the guidelines under the DILG and DBM Joint Memorandum
Circular No. 2017-1 in the utilization of the 20% development fund;
c. Require the Municipal Engineer to submit the Statement of Work Accomplished
and other necessary documents pertaining to the implementation of the above-
enumerated infrastructure projects; and
d. Require the Municipal Planning and Development Officer to conduct periodic
monitoring of the PPAs funded by the 20% development fund to ensure optimal
utilization of the fund.

We recommended that Management institute measures to hasten the liquidation of these unsettled cash advances. Also, the Municipal Accountant to maintain RPLC for each land property and PPELC for each category of property to provide its necessary information, and to conduct periodic reconciliation of records with the GSO. observations.recommendation:
Therefore, we recommended the following:
Request for a permanent personnel who will focus on the preparation of the bank reconciliation;
Prepare bank reconciliation report on a monthly basis for every depository account of the Municipality to accurately present the balances of the account in the Financial Statements.
Request for a bank snapshot which could be made available immediately or enroll in online websites of the depository banks to have an updated access of the transaction summary in aid of the reconciliation process.

During the exit conference, the Municipal Accountant explained that efforts were made to prepare the Bank Reconciliation however due to bulk transactions handled by the Accounting Office, the report was set aside. However, she promised that in the ensuing year, the bank reconciliation will be prepared.

Therefore, we recommended that the Municipal Treasurer, in as much as the accountable forms are under her responsibility to:
Demand the presentation/liquidation/return of the subject accountable forms from responsible personnel; and
Stop any releases of accountable forms to accountable officers who were with unreturned/unliquidated forms.
The management was amenable to the audit observation and promised to adhere to the audit recommendations.

Therefore, we recommended that each accountable officer should maintain Reports of Collections and Deposits properly by organizing and filing them completely to facilitate easy retrieval of records for validation and/or verification purposes.
Further, we recommended that each revenue collection clerk should maintain cashbook that will serve as permanent record of transactions for future references.
During the exit conference, the Municipal Treasurer was amenable on the audit findings and assured the audit team that in the ensuing year, cashbooks will be required to be maintained by all collecting officers.

Therefore, we recommended that the uncollected/remaining amount of the SEF Tax Receivable must not be reversed at the end of the year to present fairly the assets of the Municipality as of December 31, 2016. Further to require the Municipal Assessor to submit to the Municipal Accountant the Master list of Taxpayers of the Municipality so that the Accountant could make a sound estimate on the amount of receivable to set up at the beginning of the year.
The Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Assessor concurred on the audit findings and agreed to follow the audit recommendations. Adjustments will be effected on the 2017 financial statements.

Therefore, we recommended the following to the Municipal Accountant and the GSO Officer to ensure correct balances of the inventory account in the financial statements:
Employ the Perpetual Inventory Method
Maintain the SLC and the stock cards upon delivery of purchased inventories;
Use the Moving Average Method for costing inventories in conformity with existing regulations upon the application of the Perpetual Inventory Method.
In addition, if there were no budgetary constraints, provide bodega for the delivered inventories for storage prior to distribution/withdrawal.
The management was amenable on the audit findings and necessary action will be employed to be in accord with the audit recommendation.

Therefore, we recommended the following:
Require the submission of the completed vouchers of expenses that needs to be accrued at the end of the year from different offices concerned
Record the accrual of expenses in the books of the accountant in the period of its occurrence following the accrual accounting principle
The audit finding was seconded by the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Mayor during the exit conference. They explained that one of the reasons of the above findings was due to lack of financial resources for a particular item of expense (e.g. fuel, travel, etc.). Therefore, previous year’s receipts were presented to be paid for the current year’s budget.
It was agreed, however, that expenses exceeding the approved budget and beyond any supplemental budgets or realignments to the year it relates to, will no longer be honored.

Therefore, the audit team recommended the following:
Surcharges should not be paid out of the government funds but by the person responsible of causing the delay in the payment of utility bills. On that note, he is reminded to pay the bills on or before the due date to avoid penalties and surcharges.
Stop charging unrelated expenditures to SEF pursuant to Section 272 of the Local Government Code. Instead, the local school board should proposed programs and projects to address the emerging issues on the delivery of basic education.
Payments by the government should be done in the usual process and not by reimbursement. In this way, withholding the right taxes due the government is possible.
Strict adherence to Procurement Law is a must in all disbursements especially the prohibition of branding in government procurements.
The management was amenable to the audit finding and promised to adhere with the audit recommendations to conform to rules and regulations.

Therefore, we recommended the following in future :
Write as payee the registered name in the destination account number and not the name of the Municipal Treasurer; and
Maintain cashbooks for each PRDP accounts for easy verification of fund transactions.
The audit finding was accepted by the Management and promised to adapt the audit recommendations.

Therefore, we recommended the following:
Initiate a move to follow-up the approval of the Municipality’s 10-year plan by the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board in order to finalize and implement the programs stated therein or seek for authorization to put into operation the programs;
Convert the open dumpsite into a controlled facility. Fast track the establishment of sanitary landfill and operate the material recovery facility. Also, designate personnel that should focus on the operation of the controlled dumpsite; and
Implement the planned program/projects to address present environmental concerns of the municipality.
The management has the same opinion as the audit team and promised to undertake the audit recommendations.

Therefore, we recommended that the Accounting Office maintain sub-accounts consisting of separate sets of ledgers for each of the enterprise/special accounts and consequently prepare separate trial balances and financial statements reflecting the financial condition and results of their respective operations, in accordance with existing regulations.
The audit finding was seconded by the officials of the Municipality as it will truly help them in assessing the performance of their enterprises. Currently, all expenses of the enterprises were lump in the general expenses hence tracing of the income of the enterprise was not possible. Therefore, the Municipal Accountant promised to make the necessary ledgers to provide the needed financial statements in the ensuing year.

Therefore, we recommended that the Municipal Treasurer will maintain cashbooks for each project under the Trust Fund whether or not a separate bank account is maintained pursuant to COA Circular No. 94-013.
The Municipal Treasurer agreed to take the necessary steps in complying with the above provisions/recommendations.

Therefore, we recommended that management enjoin the Municipal accountant to strictly comply with the rules and regulations on the submission of paid disbursement vouchers including supporting documents and other Financial Reports.
Please be reminded that non-submission of these documents is sanctioned by Article 218 of the Revised Penal Code.
The management was agreeable to the finding and reasoned out that the delayed submission of the reports were mainly due to lack of personnel to handle the workloads.  It was only in the later part of CY 2016 that additional personnel were granted the Municipal Accounting Office to help in the processing of transactions in the office. Further, the conversion of accounts to PPSAS took its toll in the delay and the delayed submission of the collection reports from the Municipal Treasurer.

Commendation and Recommendations
Although, not all of the beneficiaries have been successful in the introduction of the DILEEP program, we still commend the efforts of the focal persons in implementing the program due to its undeniable impact on the lives of some marginalized beneficiaries who were granted the KABUHAYAN program.
To ensure continued success of the program in future undertaking, since there are still rooms for improvement,  we recommended the following:
The municipality should institute some interventions like skills development, business and financial management and attitude development towards earnings/business. Also, develop a reward system for those successful beneficiaries.
Strengthen the procurement process by strict adherence to the Procurement Law and indicate the specification of equipment and tools procured. An inspection of the procured items is a must before their delivery to the beneficiaries.
The concerned Municipal personnel in-charge of the DOLE projects agreed on the audit observation and assured the team that audit recommendations will be adapted to ensure the success of the DOLE projects/programs in the Municipality for the benefit of its constituents.

In view of the foregoing deficiencies we recommended the following courses of actions:
Direct the Revenue Collectors, Liquidating Officers and the Municipal Treasurer to remit and deposit their collections intact and on time pursuant to the aforecited regulations;
Require all accountable officers to properly and completely record the collections received in their respective cashbook including the actual date of collection to facilitate the determination of total accountability as of a particular date.
Direct the Municipal Treasurer-ICO to inform all collectors and barangay treasurers to remit only their collections to the Liquidating Officer, otherwise their remittances will not be recognized in audit.
The Municipal Treasurer is also reminded of the provisions of Section 104 of PD 1445 to exercise the diligence of a good father of a family in supervising accountable officers under his supervision/control to prevent the incurrence of loss of government funds or property, otherwise he shall be jointly and solidarily liable with the person primarily accountable therefor.

We recommended and Management agreed to stop the practice of procuring goods for meals and snacks thru cash advance, instead acquire the services of a legitimate caterer and follow the procurement law RA 9184 to avoid doubtful validity of procurement and risk of misuse of government funds. Also, observe strictly the provisions on the grant of cash advance as prescribed in COA Circular No. 97-002, to prevent the accumulation of unliquidated cash advance.

We recommended that Management discontinue the practice of granting cash advance/s to any official or employees without first settling or liquidation thereof has been made of the previous cash advance to prevent accumulation of unliquidated cash advances.
Also, strictly observe the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 relative to the grant, liquidation and settlement of cash advances and on the documentary requirements as laid down in COA Circular No. 2012-001, to avoid suspension of transactions.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to issue a follow-up letter to concerned barangays for immediate submission of FUR. Upon receipt of the FUR instruct the Municipal Social Worker and Development Office to validate the reported accomplishments or the completed projects of the barangays. Any deficiency should be immediately communicated to the recipient barangays for corrections. Lastly, observe strictly the provisions of COA Circular No. 94-013 to avoid non-liquidation of fund transfers to other agencies.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the Municipal Accountant to:
Identify and trace the causes of said unreconciled balances of the intra-agency accounts and effects its reconciliation; and
Maintain and regularly update subsidiary ledgers for affected accounts to support the general ledger balances’ facilitate the early detection of errors, expedite adjustments, and determine if total of the individual accounts tally with the general ledger.

We recommended that Management direct the officers of the BAFMUPCO to submit the lacking supporting documents as required in COA Circular No. 2012-001 to enable us to make a decision in audit. Strictly observe the provisions of the said circular relative to the documentary requirements for the release of funds to NGOs/POs to ensure that government funds will be protected against loss or misappropriation and to avoid suspension of transactions.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to maintain separate subsidiary ledgers for each economic enterprise in order to have adequate and reliable information not only of the ventures results of operations but also of their assets, liabilities and equity.

We recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant exercise prudence in processing obligations and disbursements, respectively, taking into account the approved budget per activity to avoid incurrence of excessive expenditures. And see to it that any increase in the budget should always be supported with approved revised or supplemental activity design with corresponding justification.
We recommended and Management agreed to stop designating the Municipal Accountant as member of TWG as this is not in accordance with COA Circular No. 2003-004.

We recommended that Management:
Require the concerned implementing office to submit the required Status of Work Accomplished, Inspection Report, pictures and Certificate of Completion and acceptance for the said infrastructure projects implemented by administration.
Henceforth, direct the Municipal Engineer to accomplish the necessary Status of Work Accomplished for every infrastructure projects implemented by administration, which will be validated by the Municipality’s Inspectorate Committee and will be the basis for the issuance of the related Certificate of Completion and Acceptance by the head of agency and attach the said documents to the disbursement vouchers to support the claims.

We recommended that Management:
Instruct the Bids and Awards Committee to adhere to its mandate which to determine if the suppliers are reputable bona fide who are duly licensed and registered for the type or categories of goods subject of the purchase are the ones that should be requested to submit price quotations to ensure competitive prices.
Strictly adhere to the provisions of Republic Act 9184 and its implementing rules and regulations in all procurement of goods, services and infrastructures, to avoid incurrence of irregular expenditures.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Treasurer to submit all paid vouchers and payrolls promptly to the Accounting Unit in order for the latter to have ample time to prepare the financial reports.  Also, observe strictly the provisions of COA Circular No. 95-006 dated May 18, 1995 and the Manual on New Government Accounting System for LGUs to ensure timely submission of financial reports.

We recommended to Management the following courses of action:
The loan amortization of the heavy equipment be transferred to the account of the MEEMDO, since these are utilized in the economic enterprise operation of the LGU. Otherwise, pull out the said units from the MEEMDO and use the equipment to implement the different development projects funded under the 20% DF to justify the charging of the loan amortization to the said fund; and
The cost of the On-Farm Mechanization Equipment should be charged to the funds of the MEEMDO if the purpose of the procurement is to earn income for the LGU, and re-appropriate the amount to other priority development projects identified by the Local Development Council.
The Management is enjoined to revisit the provisions of Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2011-1 of DILG and DBM dated April 13, 2011 to ensure that the 20% DF will be utilized to finance the priority development projects and programs that contribute to the attainment of desirable socio-economic and environmental management outcomes beneficial to the constituents.

We recommended that Management ensure that the programs, projects and activities (PPAs) being implemented under the Peace and Order and Public Safety Program of the LGU would really address the objective of the program. Also, the Management is enjoined to adhere to the provisions of the existing rules and regulations in formulating the POPSP in order to fund only those PPAs that are eligible for charging to the said fund.

We recommended that Management:
Direct the Municipal Engineer to submit the approved variation orders/extra work for evaluation by the COA RTITS.
Strictly observe the provisions of Department of Public Works and highways (DPWH) Department Order No. 22, series of 2015 and such other applicable laws, rules and regulations relative to the preparation of Approved Budget for the Contract (ABC) to avoid excessive costing of projects.

We recommend the Management direct the concerned office to fast track the completion of these projects so as not to affect or hamper the implementation of the succeeding year’s programmed projects. Also, ensure that all projects programmed under 20% DF and appropriated during the year will be fully implemented in the same year to provide timely benefits for the constituents.

We recommended that Management require the Local School Board to plan thoroughly the SEF budget for the year and submit the same promptly to the LGU to avoid delay or non-implementation of the identified priority projects and activities. Likewise, regular monitoring and reporting on the implementation should be adopted in order to address immediately the reasons for non-implementation and effect any revision to the budget, if warranted, to ensure optimal utilization of the SEF.

We reiterated our audit recommendation that Management instruct the GAD Focal Point to prepare and submit the GAD accomplishment report accompanied with its supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor for validation and tracking the resources of gender responsive and/or GAD focused policies, programs and projects based on the approved GAD plan pursuant to Joint Circular No. 2004-1.

Therefore, in view of the above observations/gaps noted, we reiterated our recommendation as last year’s:
Conduct an immediate and thorough physical count of PPE by designating personnel, who is not handling cash transactions, to do the activity.  Follow the RPCPPE format as provided in Annex 49, Volume II, LGU-NGAS Manual;
Provide property numbers on physically counted assets for identity of the properties;
Issue ARE on all properties counted, which were in the hands of accountable officers.
Retrieve all necessary documents in the transfer of completed projects under SEF or Trust Fund and transfer the same to the General Fund books. Subject the transferred property to depreciation
Maintain and update the PPELC as well as the different property ledgers.
Maintain and update property cards by the Property Custodian
Reconcile the PPELC, property cards and the physically counted properties
If budget permits, create a General Services Office (GSO) manned by competent permanent personnel who will handle the Municipality’s properties.
Further, we recommended that the Municipal properties identified above that were not included in the conversion from NGAS to PPSAS be included to present fairly the PPE accounts in the financial statements.

observations.recommendation: Therefore, we recommended that the Municipal Treasurer be dedicated in submitting the Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms and Report on obsolete, spoiled and cancelled accountable forms to trace the accuracy and completeness of the reported collection of the Collectors as well as to protect the Government for possible misappropriation of government funds.

Therefore,  we reiterated our recommendation last year that management do the following:
Exert effort to demand immediately the liquidation of all outstanding cash advances from the concerned officials/employees who are still connected to the Municipality pursuant to COA Circulars 97-002 and 2012-004.
Impose sanctions prescribed in the said Circulars for non-compliance thereof, such as withholding the salaries and other emoluments of the officials/employees who continuously disregard, fail or decline to comply with the demand, including filing appropriate charges against them.
Send demand letters to officials and employees who are no longer connected with the Municipality but whose whereabouts are still known to liquidate their cash advances and consider taking legal actions/remedy against them.
Gather all documents that will prove that liquidation of the named individuals were no longer possible such as those who are already deceased (e.g. death certificate) and whose last address was not known (e.g. retirement certification, clearances, etc.) and request for the write off of the receivable for fair presentation of the account in the Financial Statements taking into consideration the liability of those individuals who granted the clearances.
observations.reason: Demand letters were not pursued

observations.recommendation: Therefore, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant will make the proper adjustments in the books of accounts of the Municipality to reflect the correct amount of the account Other Payable – Current Portion of Long Term Debt.

Therefore, we reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the Local School Board should properly plan and implement projects to address the issues related to the delivery of basic education to the community based on the prioritization set forth by the DECS, DBM and DILG Joint Circular No. 01 series of 1998.

Further, we recommended to scrutinize all expenses requested to be charged to the SEF funds and attach documents in the disbursement vouchers to justify that disbursements were used by the schools to improve the delivery of basic education in its area and not merely used in the operation of the department/office.

Therefore, we recommended that management enjoin the Municipal accountant to strictly comply with the rules and regulations on the submission of paid disbursement vouchers including supporting documents and other Financial Reports. 

The audit team also reminded the management that non-submission of these documents is sanctioned by Article 218 of the Revised Penal Code.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the GAD Focal Point System will ensure the effective and efficient implementation of GAD PPAs and the judicious utilization of GAD Budget by properly monitoring all the PPAs. Further, it was recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer, Municipal Accountant and Heads of Implementing Departments should evaluate the propriety and validity of transactions of the PPAs before it could be charged to a specific budgeted GAD PPA.

Therefore, we recommended the management to submit the lacking documents such as Daily Time Records, Approved Application for Leave and Locator Slips. It was also recommended that the all concerned employees must comply with PD 1445 and COA Circular 2012-001 specifically on accomplishing the above supporting documents for Salaries and Wages.

We recommended that the municipality should make proper planning regarding the 20% Development Fund detailing the priority projects and appropriation for individual PPAs. The management should also closely monitor the utilization and implementation of priority projects to maximize the usage of the fund. The implementing department concerned should fast track the implementation based on the scheduled time frames indicated in the AIP to achieve the desired management outcomes.

We recommended that management should direct the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Investment Plan (LDRRMIP) together with following:
Annual Work and Financial Plan;
Programs/Projects/Activities undertaken and status thereof; and
 Report on the utilization of the LDRRMF and other dedicated disaster risk reduction and management resources.

Also, require them to strictly adhere to the provisions mentioned in the foregoing to prevent misappropriation or incurrence of loss of government funds. Various deficiencies noted in handling the Accountable Forms in the custody of Municipal Treasury Office of the Local Government of Esperanza.

Instruct the Municipal Treasurer to retrieve all stale checks and the corresponding DVs together with all supporting documents, and furnish the Municipal Accountant of the said documents for the preparation of the journal entry voucher.  Replacement checks shall be issued following the procedures in accordance with the above-cited provision.

We recommend that the Local School Board, in the preparation of the Special Education Fund Annual Budget, be guided by the provisions of existing rules and regulations so that only the authorized priority expenditures identified in the governing rules be incorporated therein to ensure proper utilization of the fund.

Enjoin Management to follow strictly the regulation in the utilization of the 5% Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund as provided under Sections 5.3 and 5.5.1 of the Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 of DBM and DILG dated March 25, 2013.

We recommend that the Management should request the Sangguniang Bayan to authorize the Municipal Treasurer to immediately transfer the total amount of Php2,625,510.04 to either Land Bank of the Philippines or Development Bank of the Philippines to protect government funds.

Instruct the Accounting Office to ensure that all claims against government funds are supported with complete documentations to avoid suspensions in audit.
Further, require the accountant and/or employee concerned to: a)submit acknowledgment receipts or any proof or evidence that the subject goods were actually received by the beneficiaries; Pictures or any other documentation shall likewise be submitted to ascertain the actual conduct of said activities; Subsequent distribution of goods to intended beneficiaries shall be completely documented with acknowledgment receipts, duly signed by the recipients; b) the Accountant to make the necessary adjusting entry; c) refrain from purchasing goods without its specific purpose; d) regulate and set a limit on the granting of mobile/cell cards allowance; and e) strictly adhere to COA Circular No. 2012-001 pertaining to cash advances requirements.

Direct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC), through its secretariat, to submit all contracts, together with all the supporting documents forming part thereof, within 5 days from its perfection to the Auditor to enable the latter to conduct auditorial review, before these contracts are forwarded to COA, Regional Office for technical evaluation.

Require the Treasury Office and Accounting Unit to strictly comply with the requirement on the submission of financial reports and transaction documents as provided in Item 7.2.1(a) of COA Circular 2009-006.

Enjoin Management to observe utmost economy by refraining from conducting LGU activities outside the locality so that other programs, projects, activities beneficial to the community can be prioritized.
Enjoin Management to refrain from incurring unnecessary expenses so that it can be used for other priority and important programs, projects, activities.

Require the Municipal Accountant to draw a journal entry voucher in the General and Trust Fund books in order to transfer the unexpended/unobligated balance of the Quick Response Fund (QRF) and the Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund - Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses (DRRMF - MOOE) in a Special Trust Fund and ensure the continued use of the fund for disaster risk reduction and management activities. 
Direct the Municipal Treasurer to transfer the amount of Php8, 214,531.79 from the General Fund to Special Trust Fund account.

Enjoin the Local Development Committee optimize the utilization of 20 percent Development and fast track the implementation of the project especially those funded out the 20% Development Fund under Continuing Appropriation.

Direct the Accounting Section to reconcile accounts with negative balances and effect necessary adjustments to reflect the correct balances for a fair presentation of the accounts in the financial statements.

Direct the General Services Officer to facilitate the immediate disposal of the unserviceable properties in accordance with Section 79 of PD 1445 and the rules and procedures provided in COA Circular 92-386, in order to prevent further deterioration and generate additional income from their disposal.
